text: A sixty-two year old man with juvenile polyposis presented with chronic anemia requiring weekly iron infusions.
He had previously undergone a laparoscopic great hemicolectomy for a bleeding polyp.
At this time, however, endoscopic workup determined that multiple gastric polyps were responsible for the bleeding, with other GI sources ruled out.
Laparoscopic total gastrectomy was recommended.
An intraoperative upper endoscopy reveals a normal gastroesophageal junction and duodenum, while the stomach is filled with numerous sessile and pedunculated polyps.
The surgeon places two 5mm and two 12mm laparoscopic ports in the upper abdomen following insufflation, preparing for the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon observes a large and bulky stomach with a transverse staple line across the antrum, indicating a previous surgical procedure. The rest of the abdominal exploration reveals normal findings.
The surgeon reflects the transverse colon cephalad, revealing and identifying the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon creates a 30-centimeter biliopancreatic limb using a 2.5 mm stapler, visibly forming a new section of the digestive tract.
The surgeon divides the mesentery using a harmonic scalpel, then measures a 45-centimeter Roux limb to ensure it can reach the hiatus without tension.
The surgeon creates a stapled jejuno-jejunostomy using a 2.5 mm stapler, connecting two segments of the jejunum with visible staple lines.
The surgeon places a Brolin anti-kinking stitch and a crotch stitch, then closes the small bowel mesenteric defect using a running silk stitch.
The surgeon uses a Nathanson liver retractor to elevate the left lateral segment of the liver, providing clear access to the stomach.
The surgeon divides all the short gastric vessels, exposing the left crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament and the frenoesophageal membrane to mobilize the esophagus, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies and transects an accessory left hepatic artery using a vascular staple load, ensuring precise division of the vessel.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the right crus and mobilizes the esophagus circumferentially, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon ligates the left gastric artery and coronary vein using a vascular staple load, securing these vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the greater curvature of the stomach, moving distally toward the pylorus, with visible separation of the stomach from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the gastroboploic vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach, clips them, and divides them using surgical instruments.
The surgeon verifies the precise location of the pyloric sphincter, ensuring accurate identification before proceeding with further surgical steps.
The surgeon reflects the antrum of the stomach upwards, carefully dividing the dense posterior adhesions connecting it to the pancreas.
The surgeon transects the duodenum just distal to the pylorus using a 3.5mm endoscopic stapler, visibly separating the duodenum from the stomach.
The surgeon completes the division of the remaining posterior attachments, fully mobilizing the stomach and freeing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places two traction sutures bilaterally in the esophagus, several centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction, to facilitate manipulation and exposure.
The surgeon transects the esophagus at the gastroesophageal junction using a 3.5mm endoscopic stapler, completing the separation of the esophagus from the stomach.
The surgeon uses an oral device to pass a 25mm EEA anvil transorally through a small esophagotomy made just posterior to the staple line.
The surgeon brings the roux limb up to the hiatus in an anticholic fashion, ensuring it reaches the site without tension.
The surgeon enlarges the left subcostal port and inserts the EEA stapler through an aneurotomy in the rhulim, bringing the spike out through the anti-mesenteric surface.
The anvil is joined to the distal esophagus, completing the anastomosis preparation.
The surgeon creates an end-to-side esophagojejunal anastomosis, connecting the transected esophagus to the jejunum.
The surgeon retrieves two complete circular tissue donuts, indicating successful creation of the esophagojejunal anastomosis with the EEA stapler.
The surgeon excises the enterotomy site on the rulem and places several reinforcing Lembert sutures between the jejunum and esophagus, ensuring secure anastomosis.
The surgeon occludes the Rulim with a bowel clamp and performs an air leak test, confirming no air leakage from the anastomosis site.
The surgeon marks the jejunum fifteen centimeters distal to the enterostomy site for the placement of a feeding jejunostomy tube.
The surgeon connects the two left subcostal port sites to create a larger incision for extracting the bulky specimen. A Witzel jejunostomy is then created through this incision.
The patient was kept NPO until upper gastrointestinal series on postoperative day five showed no evidence of a leak.
At that time, his diet was advanced and he was discharged uneventfully.
Final pathology confirmed the presence of juvenile polyposis without any evidence of dysplasia.
